agent1_snippets.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Sealers
agent1_snippets.content: Bioceramic sealers are biocompatible materials that use either calcium silicate or calcium phosphate as their main constituent. [21] Mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA) is a new material currently used as a root canal sealer, in addition to its many other uses within the field of endodontics. The formula of MTA is primarily tricalcium silicate with added bismuth oxide, a radiopaque powder for enhanced visualization on radiography. [21] [22] MTA is activated with the introduction of water and produces a highly alkaline mixture with a pH of 12 and beneficial calcium silicate hydrates. These hydrates make MTA an excellent endodontic material as they form on the surface of existing calcium silicate particles and continue to grow inwards, effectively creating a barrier. [21] [22] One major disadvantage of bioceramic sealers is that the enhanced adhesion between the material and the dentin wall makes endodontic retreatment and post-preparation increasingly challenging. [21] [22]

agent1_snippets.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Definition/Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Biodentine has emerged in recent years as an innovative bioactive dentine substitute. Its superior physical properties, improved handling, and various clinical applications have made biodentine popular in dentistry. It has been advocated for vital pulp therapy, root perforations, resorptions, retrograde fillings, and dentin replacement. Biodentine is calcium silicate-based cement formed by highly purified tricalcium silicate (80.1%). [1] Unlike mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA), it does not contain bismuth oxide, which is known to delay setting, affecting biocompatibility and causing discoloration. [1]

agent1_snippets.content: Research into pulp biology and the regenerative potential of inflamed pulp has supported the adoption of VPT in select cases. Histological studies indicate that inflammation is often confined to specific pulp areas near the exposure site, with normal histological architecture found in other pulp parts. [6]  Clinical symptoms of irreversible pulpitis do not always align with histological findings, emphasizing the need to revise diagnostic terminology and implement minimally invasive treatments. [6]

agent1_snippets.content: Another significant application of hydroxyapatite in dentistry dates back to 1979 when hydroxyapatite cylinders were used for tooth replacement. This application was followed in the early 1980s by the use of hydroxyapatite blocks and coatings to enhance bone fixation in restorative dental procedures. Hydroxyapatite is now used not only in dental cement and fillings but also in toothpaste, where it serves as a polisher to reduce the buildup of accretions on teeth. [14] [7]

agent1_snippets.title: Dental Emergencies -- Treatment / Management -- Tooth Fractures
agent1_snippets.content: The focus of initial treatment for enamel-dentin fractures is to protect the exposed dentin. Calcium hydroxide liners are commonly available in urgent care clinics, emergency departments, and primary care offices. These healthcare providers should apply this temporary treatment to prevent infection and protect the tooth’s viability. [58] In dental offices, calcium hydroxide can also be applied initially; however, the material must eventually be covered with a composite resin or glass ionomer. If the fragment is available, a dentist can consider reattaching it. If the fragment is unavailable, the dentist can perform restorative procedures using various materials, including resin direct composites or ceramics. [49]

agent1_snippets.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Applications of Biodentine: Pulpotomy in Permanent Teeth with Symptoms of Irreversible Pulpitis
agent1_snippets.content: Vital pulp therapy (VPT) is a minimally invasive approach for managing teeth with inflamed pulps compared to conventional root canal treatment (RCT). Several factors have contributed to the growing popularity of VPT, including the limitations of root canal treatments despite advancements in technology, the advantages of preserving tooth structure, maintaining possible defensive mechanisms of the remaining pulp, and reducing the risk of tooth fracture. [6] Additionally, VPT has shown comparable success rates to RCT over a 5-year follow-up. [6]

agent1_snippets.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Gutta-Percha
agent1_snippets.content: Gutta-percha has many advantages, such as ease of manipulation, radiopacity, low toxicity, and plasticity. However, it does not bond to dentin and shrinks when cooled. [5] [25] Gutta-percha cones are available in standardized 0.02 taper as specified by the International Organization of Standardization and non-standardized sizes. [5] This allows clinicians to mechanically prepare the canal space using various commercial rotary systems using standardized file sizes. When the canal is prepared to a given file size, a gutta-percha cone of the same size can be inserted and adapted to the canal. [1] [5] [25]

agent1_snippets.content: The study results show high success rates for Biodentine and formocresol pulpotomy techniques over 12 months, which can be attributed to proper protocol, isolation, aseptic conditions, and material handling. [5] The success rates were consistent with earlier research, emphasizing that Biodentine can be a suitable alternative for formocresol in primary teeth pulpotomy. [5] Furthermore, the simultaneous use of Biodentine as a dressing and restorative material offers a clinical advantage over formocresol, which requires additional restorative material in the pulp chamber. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Applications of Biodentine: Primary Teeth Pulpotomy
agent1_snippets.content: Pulpotomy is a procedure to preserve the function and vitality of the radicular pulp by surgically removing the coronal part of an exposed vital pulp. Formocresol has traditionally been used because of its clinical success and ease of use, but concern has been raised regarding mutagenic, toxic, and carcinogenic risks. Biodentine has been suggested as an alternative material to formocresol for pulpotomy procedures. A 2019 randomized clinical trial evaluated the use of Biodentine for pulpotomy in primary molars in children, comparing its clinical and radiographic success rates with formocresol. [5] Formocresol was chosen due to its long-term clinical success despite concerns over adverse reactions. MTA, another alternative, presented challenges in handling and setting time.

agent1_snippets.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Calcium Hydroxide Sealers
agent1_snippets.content: Calcium hydroxide is a widely used material in clinical dentistry due to its therapeutic properties for dental pulp in procedures such as pulp capping and apexification. [11] [12] The key therapeutic properties behind its widespread use are its ability to promote the healing of periapical tissues and its antimicrobial effect. [13] The exact process through which calcium hydroxide exerts its action is still unknown, but some mechanisms have been proposed. Calcium hydroxide is highly alkaline (pH of 12), which is believed to trigger reparative and remineralization activities. It may also denature specific intracanal proteins and arrest external root resorption, promoting healing. [9] [13] [12] [9] However, the exact mechanism of action of calcium hydroxide remains under investigation. Several calcium hydroxide-based sealers are available, and they vary in antibacterial effect, cytotoxicity, and solubility. [13] [12] Calcium hydroxide-based sealers have lower cytotoxicity compared to other groups of sealers. [13]  Their sealing capacity to provide an appropriate seal has been proven similar to that of ZOE sealers. [14] Solubility could be a concern. [13]

agent1_snippets.content: Direct pulp capping with Biodentine has been explored in various clinical scenarios. Direct pulp capping protects exposed pulp and promotes pulp vitality by forming tertiary dentin. [1] The prognosis of direct pulp capping depends on the type of pulp exposure, with traumatic or iatrogenic exposures having better outcomes than carious exposures. [1] Recent bioactive materials, such as Biodentine, have improved the predictability of the procedure following carious exposures.

agent1_snippets.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Contraindications
agent1_snippets.content: There are few true contraindications for sealers and core obturation materials. One major contraindication is an allergy to any component of the material. [35] Allergies to constituent materials are rare but possible in every sealer and core material discussed, and the operator should take care to review all of a patient's possible allergies and be able to respond should a medical emergency arise. Other non-absolute contraindications are case-specific and depend on the treatment goal of the operator.

agent1_snippets.title: Hyperphosphatemic Tumoral Calcinosis -- History and Physical -- Dental Involvement
agent1_snippets.content: Teeth are involved very commonly in HTC. The various dental defects reported include pulp chamber obliteration, pulp stones, shortening of roots with bulbous enlargement in the middle, and enamel changes. Besides, a peculiar thistle shape of roots has been described. Also, the roots have often been found curved, a process called dilaceration. These dental changes are most commonly seen in premolar teeth. [18] The importance of dental involvement lies in its early recognition by dentists, as often these are the first reported manifestations of HTC before the development of tumoral calcinosis. Thus, appropriate evaluative, preventive, and treatment measures can be initiated once these changes are identified.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1916
agent2_snippets.content: Calcium acetate and calcium phosphate dibasic are both classified as calcium compounds. As members of the broader calcium compounds category, they share the fundamental property of containing calcium ions, yet they are applied differently in medical practice. Calcium acetate is frequently used as a phosphate binder for patients with chronic kidney disease, helping to control serum phosphate levels, while calcium phosphate dibasic serves as a calcium source in nutritional supplements and supports bone health. Recognizing that both agents belong to the calcium compounds group aids clinicians in understanding their chemical characteristics and therapeutic roles.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
agent2_snippets.content: Calcium compounds constitute a class of chemical substances that contain calcium, and this class includes calcium glycerophosphate, calcium acetate, and calcium oxide. Calcium glycerophosphate is a calcium‑phosphate salt that can serve as a source of both minerals. Calcium acetate is commonly employed as a phosphate‑binding agent in patients with renal failure to reduce serum phosphate levels. Calcium oxide, also known as quicklime, can be used as a calcium supplement or antacid in various medical contexts. All three substances are categorized under the broader term “Calcium Compounds,” reflecting their shared elemental composition while each possesses distinct chemical properties and clinical applications.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
agent2_snippets.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group. It is employed therapeutically to correct نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia), reflecting its central role in raising serum calcium levels. Calcitonin is described as having a relationship with parathormon, highlighting the complementary and often opposing actions of these two hormones in the regulation of calcium metabolism. Understanding the classification of parathormon, its use in treating hypocalcemia, and its interaction with calcitonin is essential for managing disorders of calcium balance and for guiding appropriate patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1915
agent2_snippets.content: Bulk compounding chemicals refer to the inorganic substances used in large‑scale pharmaceutical preparation, and this category includes both calcium oxide and tricalcium phosphate. Calcium oxide is a basic oxide that can be employed to adjust pH or provide calcium ions in compounded formulations. Tricalcium phosphate, on the other hand, serves as a calcium and phosphate source and is commonly used as a filler or binder in oral dosage forms. By recognizing calcium oxide and tricalcium phosphate as members of the bulk compounding chemicals group, formulators can select appropriate compounds to achieve desired physicochemical properties and therapeutic outcomes in compounded medications.
